test_id_1.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

compare_sections.headline_summary: According to the newest policy source dated 2023-04-01, dual eligible pharmacy claims are handled by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug Contracting (MAPD) plan. The provider manual guidance from the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines dated 2025-10-01 states that all prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis.
compare_sections.policy_definition: [Document Title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no4_mar23_speced_pr.pdf::0003] — 2023-03-01 — [pages: [2, 3]]

[Document Title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no4_mar23_speced_pr.pdf::0003] — 2023-03-01 — [pages: [2, 3]]

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0125] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [30, 31]]
comparison: Similarity: NYRx provides prescription drug coverage for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members.
[Document Title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no4_mar23_speced_pr.pdf::0003] — 2023-03-01 — [pages: [2, 3]]

Difference: The provider manual states that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. This is not explicitly stated in the policy source.
[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0125] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [30, 31]]
compare_sections.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy.

policy.text:
2

Pharmacy Providers
Dual Eligible Coverage
For NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug
Contracting (MAPD) plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for the drugs listed below. To
view all drugs covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members, providers can refer to the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) “Drugs Covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible Members Effective 10/22/2020” web
page, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/medicare
_exempt_drugs.htm. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.
For the most up to date coverage information for the products below, providers should refer to the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/fullform.pdf.
Prescription Drugs:
• Vitamins
Folic acid – oral
Hydroxocobalamin – injection
Vitamin B12 – injection/nasal
Vitamin D2 – oral
Vitamin K – oral
• Cough and Cold
• Benzonatate
Please note: NYRx does not provide dual eligible members with coverage of compounded
prescriptions.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.answer:
{
  "headline_summary": "According to the newest policy source dated 2023-04-01, dual eligible pharmacy claims are handled by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug Contracting (MAPD) plan. The provider manual guidance from the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines dated 2025-10-01 states that all prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis.",
  "policy_definition": "[Document Title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no4_mar23_speced_pr.pdf::0003] — 2023-03-01 — [pages: [2, 3]]",
  "provider_manual_definition": "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0125] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [30, 31]]",
  "similarities": ["Similarity: NYRx provides prescription drug coverage for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members.", "[Document Title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no4_mar23_speced_pr.pdf::0003] — 2023-03-01 — [pages: [2, 3]]"],
  "differences": ["Difference: The provider manual states that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. This is not explicitly stated in the policy source.", "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0125] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [30, 31]]"],
  "caveats": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy."
}
test_id_1.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_1.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_1.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

policy.text:
2

Pharmacy Providers
Dual Eligible Coverage
For NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug
Contracting (MAPD) plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for the drugs listed below. To
view all drugs covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members, providers can refer to the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) “Drugs Covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible Members Effective 10/22/2020” web
page, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/medicare
_exempt_drugs.htm. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.
For the most up to date coverage information for the products below, providers should refer to the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/fullform.pdf.
Prescription Drugs:
• Vitamins
Folic acid – oral
Hydroxocobalamin – injection
Vitamin B12 – injection/nasal
Vitamin D2 – oral
Vitamin K – oral
• Cough and Cold
• Benzonatate
Please note: NYRx does not provide dual eligible members with coverage of compounded
prescriptions.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

{
  "headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, all prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis for dual eligible pharmacy claims. In contrast, the policy guidance from New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8 states that pharmacy claims should be referred to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy.",
  "policy_definition": "[Document Title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no4_mar23_speced_pr.pdf::0003] — 2023-03-01 — [pages: [2, 3]]",
  "provider_manual_definition": "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0125] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [30, 31]]",
  "similarities": ["Similarity: NYRx provides prescription drug coverage for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members.", "[Document Title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no4_mar23_speced_pr.pdf::0003] — 2023-03-01 — [pages: [2, 3]]"],
  "differences": ["Difference: The provider manual states that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. This is not explicitly stated in the policy source.", "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0125] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [30, 31]]"],

test_id_3.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_3.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_3.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can

• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_5.answer:
{
  "headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, all prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis for dual eligible pharmacy claims. In contrast, the policy guidance from New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8 states that pharmacy claims should be referred to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy.",
  "policy_definition": "[Document Title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no4_mar23_speced_pr.pdf::0003] — 2023-03-01 — [pages: [2, 3]]",
  "provider_manual_definition": "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0069] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [18]]",
  "similarities": ["Similarity: NYRx provides prescription drug coverage for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members.", "[Document Title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4] — [Chunk ID: mu_no4_mar23_speced_pr.pdf::0003] — 2023-03-01 — [pages: [2, 3]]"],
  "differences": ["Difference: The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient.", "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0069] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [18]]"],
  "caveats": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided policy chunks to define this concept for current guidance."
}
test_id_5.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_5.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_5.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

compare_sections.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims is that the pharmacy provider must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, with NYS Medicaid being the payer of last resort. In contrast, policy guidance from New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4 states that pharmacies are responsible for timely and appropriate billing to facilities, commercial plans, Medicare, or Medicaid.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
For dual-eligible members (in receipt of both Medicare and NYS Medicaid) who have enrolled in the hospice
program, NYS DOH will use a file supplied by Medicare to load the RR/E code C2 in eMedNY on the members
NYS Medicaid record.
For non-dual NYS Medicaid members, RR/E code C2 will be manually added to the record of the NYS
Medicaid member by NYS DOH upon receipt of the Hospice Care Recipient Restriction/Exception (RR/E)
Code Update Form, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/redesign/mrt90/mltc
_policy/2023/docs/att1.pdf, from an LDSS, MMCO, or hospice provider. If a NYS Medicaid member in
receipt of hospice services elects to discontinue hospice services, RR/E code C2 must be manually
closed by NYS DOH.
Due to limitations of data received from Medicare, NYS Medicaid members no longer in receipt of hospice
care will need to have RR/E code C2 manually closed with a manually entered end-date.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.

Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
For dual-eligible members (in receipt of both Medicare and NYS Medicaid) who have enrolled in the hospice
program, NYS DOH will use a file supplied by Medicare to load the RR/E code C2 in eMedNY on the members
NYS Medicaid record.
For non-dual NYS Medicaid members, RR/E code C2 will be manually added to the record of the NYS
Medicaid member by NYS DOH upon receipt of the Hospice Care Recipient Restriction/Exception (RR/E)
Code Update Form, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/redesign/mrt90/mltc
_policy/2023/docs/att1.pdf, from an LDSS, MMCO, or hospice provider. If a NYS Medicaid member in
receipt of hospice services elects to discontinue hospice services, RR/E code C2 must be manually
closed by NYS DOH.
Due to limitations of data received from Medicare, NYS Medicaid members no longer in receipt of hospice
care will need to have RR/E code C2 manually closed with a manually entered end-date.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_13.answer:
{
  "headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims is that pharmacies must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, with NYS Medicaid being the payer of last resort. This guidance supersedes previous policy guidance from New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5, which states that providers are responsible for timely and appropriate billing to facilities, commercial plans, Medicare, or Medicaid. The provider manual is more recent than the policy guidance, so it should receive more weight by default.",
  "policy_definition": "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5] — [Chunk ID: mu_no5_may24_pr.pdf::0007] — 2024-05-01 — [pages: [3]]",
  "provider_manual_definition": "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0165] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [42]]",
  "similarities": ["Similarity: NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort.", "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5] — [Chunk ID: mu_no5_may24_pr.pdf::0007] — 2024-05-01 — [pages: [3]]"],
  "differences": ["Difference: Providers must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, whereas the policy guidance states that providers are responsible for timely and appropriate billing to facilities, commercial plans, Medicare, or Medicaid.", "[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5] — [Chunk ID: mu_no5_may24_pr.pdf::0007] — 2024-05-01 — [pages: [3]]"],
  "caveats": ""
}
test_id_13.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_13.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_13.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_14.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

compare_sections.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims is that pharmacies must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, with NYS Medicaid being the payer of last resort. This guidance supersedes previous policy guidance from New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5, which states that providers are responsible for timely and appropriate billing to facilities, commercial plans, Medicare, or Medicaid. The provider manual is more recent than the policy guidance, so it should receive more weight by default.
compare_sections.policy_definition: [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5] — [Chunk ID: mu_no5_may24_pr.pdf::0007] — 2024-05-01 — [pages: [3]]
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: [Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] — [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0165] — 2025-10-01 — [pages: [42]]
similarities: According to the policy and provider manual, it is the responsibility of the provider to submit claims with their usual or customary fee indicated.
Both sources agree that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
differences: The policy states that providers are responsible for timely and appropriate billing to facilities, commercial plans, Medicare, or Medicaid, while the provider manual states that pharmacies must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid.
The policy does not explicitly state that pharmacies must bill third parties before billing Medicaid.
comparison: According to the policy and provider manual, it is the responsibility of the provider to submit claims with their usual or customary fee indicated.
Both sources agree that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

The policy states that providers are responsible for timely and appropriate billing to facilities, commercial plans, Medicare, or Medicaid, while the provider manual states that pharmacies must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid.
The policy does not explicitly state that pharmacies must bill third parties before billing Medicaid.

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
